message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The involvement of SARS-CoV-2 antibodies in mediating immunopathogenetic events in COVID-19 patients has been suggested. By using several experimental approaches, we investigated the potential association between SARS-CoV-2 IgGs recognizing the spike (S) protein receptor-binding domain (RBD), neutralizing antibodies (NtAb) targeting S, and COVID-19 severity.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Patients and Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    This unicenter, retrospective, observational study included 51 hospitalized patients (24 at the intensive care unit; ICU). A total of 93 sera from these patients collected at different time points from the onset of symptoms were analyzed. SARS-CoV-2 RBD IgGs were quantitated by ELISA and NtAb
                    <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                    titers were measured in a GFP reporter-based pseudotyped virus platform. Demographic and clinical data, complete blood counts, as well as serum levels of ferritin, Dimer-D, C reactive protein (CRP), lactose dehydrogenase (LDH), and interleukin-6 (IL-6) were retrieved from clinical charts.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The overall correlation between levels of both antibody measurements was good (Rho=0.79;
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    =0<0.001). SARS-CoV-2 RBD IgG and NtAb
                    <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                    levels in sera collected up to day 30 after the onset of symptoms were comparable between ICU and non-ICU patients (
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    =>0.1). The percentage of patients who exhibited high NtAb
                    <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                    titers (≥ 160) was similar (
                    <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                    =0.20) in ICU (79%) and non-ICU (60%) patients. Four ICU patients died; two of these achieved NtAb
                    <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                    titers ≥ 1/160 while the other two exhibited a 1/80 titer. Very weak (Rho=>0.0-<0.2) or weak (Rho=>0.2-<0.4) correlations were observed between anti-RBD IgGs, NtAb
                    <jats:sub>50,</jats:sub>
                    and serum levels pro-inflammatory biomarkers.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The data presented herein do not support an association between SARS-CoV-2 RBD IgG or NtAb
                    <jats:sub>50</jats:sub>
                    levels and COVID-19 severity.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: SARS-CoV-2 antibodies, serum inflammatory biomarkers and clinical severity of hospitalized COVID-19 Patients
